preamble.summary: Abram goes to Egypt during famine, where Sarai's beauty endangers them. After returning, Abram and Lot separate — Lot choosing the Jordan plain, Abram remaining in Canaan. God renews the land promise. Abram settles at Hebron and builds an altar. He gives tithes to God, establishing the tithing institution.
preamble.remarkable: The Egyptian sojourn (Genesis 12:10-20) is retold with Abram emerging more sympathetically — the deception about Sarai is toned down. The tithe passage (vv. 25-29) is a major addition: Abram's tithing establishes the practice as a patriarchal institution predating Levitical law. The Feast of Firstfruits is observed by Abram, linking his worship to the later festival calendar.
preamble.friction: The Genesis account of Abram's deception in Egypt is sanitized in Jubilees — the moral complexity of Genesis 12:13 is reduced. The tithing section reads later Levitical practice back into the patriarchal period.
preamble.connections: Genesis 12:10-20 (Abram in Egypt); Genesis 13 (separation from Lot); Genesis 13:14-17 (land promise renewed); Leviticus 27:30-33 (tithing law); Numbers 18:21-32 (Levitical tithe); Hebrews 7:1-10 (Abram's tithe to Melchizedek, ch. 13:25-27 anticipates ch. 14).

verses.text_reference: And Abram went up from Haran to the land of Canaan, he and Sarai his wife and Lot his brother's son.
verses.rendering: Abram went from Haran to the land of Canaan — he, Sarai his wife, and Lot his nephew.
translator_notes: A summary transition linking the departure narrative to the events in Canaan.

verses.text_reference: And there was a famine in the land, and Abram went into Egypt in the third year of the famine.
verses.rendering: There was a famine in the land, and Abram went to Egypt in the third year of the famine.
translator_notes: The famine drives Abram south to Egypt — the first of several descents to Egypt in Genesis. Three years of famine before the journey intensifies the desperation.

verses.text_reference: And he dwelt in Egypt five years before his wife was torn away from him.
verses.rendering: He lived in Egypt for five years before his wife was taken from him.
translator_notes: Five years before Sarai is taken — a much longer stay than Genesis implies. This extended sojourn means Abram had an established life in Egypt when the crisis struck.

verses.text_reference: Now Tanis in Egypt was at that time built — seven years after Hebron.
verses.rendering: Now Tanis in Egypt was built at that time — seven years after Hebron.
translator_notes: A chronological note matching Numbers 13:22. This detail anchors the narrative in verifiable Egyptian history.

verses.text_reference: And it came to pass when Pharaoh seized Sarai, the wife of Abram, that the Lord plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife.
verses.rendering: When Pharaoh seized Sarai, Abram's wife, the LORD struck Pharaoh and his household with severe plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife.
translator_notes: Genesis 12:17. God intervenes to protect the covenant wife. The plagues foreshadow the Exodus plagues — Egypt is punished for taking what belongs to God's chosen.

verses.text_reference: And Abram was very glorious by reason of possessions in sheep, and cattle, and asses, and horses, and camels, and menservants, and maidservants, and in silver and gold exceedingly. And Lot also, his brother's son, was wealthy.
verses.rendering: Abram became very wealthy — possessing sheep, cattle, donkeys, horses, camels, male and female servants, and great quantities of silver and gold. Lot, his nephew, was also wealthy.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:2, 5. Prosperity in Egypt despite the crisis — God's blessing operates even in foreign lands. The wealth list is comprehensive, establishing Abram as a major figure.

verses.text_reference: And Pharaoh restored Sarai, the wife of Abram, and he sent him out of the land of Egypt, and he journeyed to the place where he had pitched his tent at the beginning, to the place of the altar, with Ai on the east and Bethel on the west, and he blessed the Lord his God who had brought him back in peace.
verses.rendering: Pharaoh restored Sarai, Abram's wife, and sent him out of Egypt. He journeyed to the place where he had pitched his tent at the beginning — the place of the altar, with Ai to the east and Bethel to the west — and he blessed the LORD his God who had brought him back in peace.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:1-4. Abram returns to his original altar site — the place of worship is not merely geographical but relational, marking where he first encountered God in Canaan.

verses.text_reference: And in the fourth year of this week Lot parted from him, and Lot dwelt in Sodom, and the men of Sodom were sinners exceedingly.
verses.rendering: In the fourth year of that week, Lot separated from him. Lot settled in Sodom, and the men of Sodom were exceedingly sinful.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:12-13. The separation is presented economically. Lot's choice of Sodom is immediately qualified by the city's extreme sinfulness — an ominous foreshadowing.

verses.text_reference: And it grieved him in his heart that his brother's son had parted from him; for he had no children.
verses.rendering: It grieved Abram deeply that his nephew had separated from him, for he had no children.
translator_notes: Lot was Abram's surrogate son — his departure leaves Abram not only alone but without an heir. Childlessness intensifies the pain of separation.

verses.text_reference: In that year when Lot was taken captive, God said to Abram, after that Lot had parted from him, in the fourth year of this week: 'Lift up thine eyes from the place where thou art dwelling, northward and southward, and westward and eastward.
verses.rendering: In that year when Lot was taken captive, after Lot had separated from him — in the fourth year of that week — God said to Abram, 'Lift up your eyes from the place where you are living — to the north and south, west and east.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:14-15. God's promise comes after loss — when Lot departs, God enlarges the vision. The four directional compass mirrors the scope of the promise.

verses.text_reference: For all the land which thou seest I will give to thee and to thy seed for ever, and I will make thy seed as the sand of the sea: though a man may number the dust of the earth, yet thy seed shall not be numbered.
verses.rendering: All the land you see I will give to you and your descendants forever. I will make your descendants like the sand of the sea — even if someone could count the dust of the earth, your descendants could not be numbered.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:15-16. The promise is doubled: the land is 'forever' and the descendants are innumerable. Both claims require faith, since Abram is currently childless and landless.

verses.text_reference: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and the breadth of it, and see it all; for to thy seed will I give it.' And Abram went to Hebron, and dwelt there.
verses.rendering: Arise, walk through the land — its length and breadth — and see it all, for I will give it to your descendants.' Abram went to Hebron and settled there.
translator_notes: Genesis 13:17-18. Walking the land is an act of symbolic possession — Abram surveys what God has promised. Hebron becomes his home base.

verses.text_reference: And in this year came Chedorlaomer, king of Elam, and Amraphel, king of Shinar, and Arioch, king of Sellasar, and Tidal, king of nations, and they slew the king of Gomorrah, and the king of Sodom fled, and many fell through wounds in the vale of Siddim, by the Salt Sea.
verses.rendering: That year Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, and Tidal king of nations came and killed the king of Gomorrah. The king of Sodom fled, and many fell wounded in the Valley of Siddim by the Salt Sea.
translator_notes: Genesis 14:1-10 condensed. The war of the four kings against five — Abram's first engagement with international warfare. The Salt Sea is the Dead Sea.

verses.text_reference: And they took captive Sodom and Adam and Zeboim, and they took captive Lot also, the son of Abram's brother, and all his possessions, and they went to Dan.
verses.rendering: They captured Sodom, Admah, and Zeboiim, and they also captured Lot — Abram's nephew — with all his possessions. They went to Dan.
translator_notes: Genesis 14:11-14. Lot's capture draws Abram into the conflict. The connection between Lot's earlier choice (Sodom) and his current suffering is implicit.

verses.text_reference: And one who had escaped came and told Abram that his brother's son had been taken captive and Abram armed his household servants.
verses.rendering: A fugitive came and told Abram that his nephew had been taken captive. Abram mobilized his household servants.
translator_notes: Genesis 14:13-14. Abram acts immediately — family loyalty overrides the earlier separation. His 'household servants' are a private militia of 318 trained men (Genesis 14:14).

verses.text_reference: And he went after them unto Dan, and he found them encamping in Dan, and fell upon them by night and pursued them and smote them and slew them, and recovered all their spoil and saved his brother's son and his possessions.
verses.rendering: He pursued them to Dan and found them encamped at Dan. He attacked them at night, pursued them, struck them down, and killed them. He recovered all the plunder and rescued his nephew and his possessions.
translator_notes: A night raid — military surprise. Abram defeats the coalition of four kings with household troops, a stunning military achievement. Dan is the northernmost point of the later Israelite territory.

verses.text_reference: And the king of Sodom came to him and bowed himself before him, and said: 'Our Lord Abram, give unto us the persons whom thou hast saved, but let the booty be thine.'
verses.rendering: The king of Sodom came to him and bowed before him, saying, 'Our lord Abram, give us the people you rescued, and keep the plunder for yourself.'
translator_notes: Genesis 14:21. The king of Sodom's offer is a test: will Abram be enriched by Sodom? He will refuse (in line with Genesis 14:22-23).

verses.text_reference: And Abram said to him: 'I lift up my hands to the Most High God, that I will not take from anything that is thine, not from a thread to a shoelatchet, lest thou shouldst say, I have made Abram rich; save only what the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. These shall take their portion.'
verses.rendering: Abram said to him, 'I raise my hands to the Most High God — I will take nothing of yours, not a thread or a sandal strap, so that you cannot say, "I made Abram rich." Only what the young men have eaten and the share of the men who went with me — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre — they may take their portion.'
translator_notes: Genesis 14:22-24. Abram refuses to profit from Sodom — his wealth comes from God alone, not from a wicked city. His allies receive their fair share, but Abram himself takes nothing.

verses.text_reference: And he returned all the goods of Sodom, and Lot his brother also went with him to his place of sojourn, and Abram returned also to his place.
verses.rendering: He returned all the goods of Sodom. Lot his nephew also went back to his place of residence, and Abram returned to his home.
translator_notes: Lot goes back to Sodom despite everything — a tragic detail. Having been rescued from the consequences of his choice, he returns to the same choice.

verses.text_reference: And God appeared to him in a vision, saying: 'Be not afraid, Abram: I am thy defender, and thy reward shall be exceeding great.'
verses.rendering: God appeared to him in a vision, saying, 'Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, and your reward will be exceedingly great.'
translator_notes: Genesis 15:1. After refusing Sodom's wealth, God promises to be Abram's 'shield' (protector) and 'reward' (compensation). The refusal of earthly reward is met with divine reward.

verses.text_reference: And Abram said, 'Lord, Lord, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go hence childless, and the son of Maseq, the son of my handmaid, is the Dammeseq Eliezer: unto me thou hast given no seed.'
verses.rendering: Abram said, 'Lord, LORD, what will you give me? I remain childless, and the son of Maseq, the son of my servant — Eliezer of Damascus — what can you give me since you have given me no offspring?'
translator_notes: Genesis 15:2-3. Abram's complaint is raw and honest — all the promises of land and greatness are meaningless without an heir. His steward Eliezer stands to inherit everything.

verses.text_reference: And He said to him: 'This (man) will not be thine heir, but one that will come out of thine own body will be thine heir.'
verses.rendering: God said to him, 'This man will not be your heir. One who comes from your own body will be your heir.'
translator_notes: Genesis 15:4. The promise is biological, not adoptive — Abram's own son will inherit. This promise precedes both Ishmael and Isaac.

verses.text_reference: And He brought him forth abroad, and said unto him: 'Look toward heaven and number the stars if thou art able to number them.' And he looked toward heaven, and beheld the stars. And He said unto him: 'So shall thy seed be.'
verses.rendering: He brought him outside and said, 'Look toward heaven and count the stars, if you can count them.' Abram looked toward heaven and saw the stars. God said to him, 'So will your descendants be.'
translator_notes: Genesis 15:5. One of the most iconic scenes in Scripture — the stargazing promise. The stars are innumerable; so will Abram's descendants be.

verses.text_reference: And he believed in the Lord, and it was counted to him for righteousness.
verses.rendering: He believed in the LORD, and it was counted to him as righteousness.
translator_notes: Genesis 15:6 — one of the most important verses in biblical theology. Faith precedes and produces righteousness. Paul (Romans 4, Galatians 3) and James (James 2:23) both build their theology on this verse.

verses.text_reference: And he celebrated the feast of the first fruits of the grain harvest in this year, and he offered new offerings on the altar, the first fruits of the produce of the field.
verses.rendering: He celebrated the feast of the firstfruits of the grain harvest that year, offering new offerings on the altar — the firstfruits of the field's produce.
translator_notes: Abram observes the Feast of Firstfruits — another patriarchal precedent for a festival later codified at Sinai. The agricultural offering connects Abram to the land's productivity.

verses.text_reference: And he tithed all that was clean among his cattle, and made offerings thereof; and he offered all the fat on the altar, and selected the choicest for God.
verses.rendering: He tithed everything clean among his cattle and made offerings. He placed all the fat on the altar and selected the finest portions for God.
translator_notes: Abram's tithe is comprehensive — all clean animals are tithed. This establishes the practice before the Levitical system and before Melchizedek (who will receive a tithe in ch. 13/14). The 'choicest for God' principle governs all sacrifice.

verses.text_reference: And he offered a thank-offering of wine and fine flour, and frankincense, and he offered them on the altar, as a sacrifice of pleasant savour.
verses.rendering: He offered a thanksgiving of wine and fine flour and frankincense, placing them on the altar as a sacrifice of pleasing aroma.
translator_notes: A thanksgiving offering combining wine, flour, and incense — the complete complement of non-animal offerings. Gratitude drives worship.

verses.text_reference: And he blessed the Lord who had created heaven and earth, who had made all the fat things of the earth, and had given them to the children of men that they might eat and drink and bless their Creator.
verses.rendering: He blessed the LORD who created heaven and earth, who made all the good things of the earth and gave them to humanity so they might eat, drink, and bless their Creator.
translator_notes: The theology of blessing: God creates good things, gives them to humanity, and humanity responds with gratitude. This cycle of gift and thanksgiving is the heartbeat of Jubilees' worship theology.

verses.text_reference: And now I render thanks unto thee, my God, because thou hast caused me to see this day. Behold I am sixty-five years of age, and I know the Lord and I seek him and follow after him, and I have a righteous heart and an upright mind. And that thou wouldest prosper me in all my paths.'
verses.rendering: And now I give thanks to you, my God, because you have let me see this day. I am sixty-five years old, and I know the LORD and seek him and follow him. I have a righteous heart and an upright mind. May you cause me to prosper in all my paths.'
translator_notes: Abram's personal testimony at sixty-five: he knows God, seeks him, and follows him. The prayer for prosperity is not materialistic but vocational — he asks to fulfill his calling.
